title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about volunteering and charity work. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: What We Give
content.subtitle: The psychology of voluntary work and why it matters
content.text:
The popular narrative around volunteering tends to focus on what is given — time, skills, effort — while saying relatively little about what is received. [31]Yet research consistently shows that regular volunteers report higher levels of personal wellbeing than non-volunteers, a finding that holds across age groups, income levels, and cultural backgrounds, suggesting that the act of helping others may benefit the helper as much as those helped.[/31]

Dr Amara Nwosu, a social psychologist at the University of Leeds, has spent a decade studying the relationship between voluntary work and psychological health. [32]Her research distinguishes between two types of volunteer motivation: what she calls 'contributive' motivation, driven by a genuine desire to help, and 'resume' motivation, driven primarily by the desire to add experience to a job application. Her findings suggest that these two types of motivation produce quite different psychological outcomes.[/32]

[33]Volunteers motivated primarily by contributive goals — those who chose roles because they cared about the cause — showed sustained improvements in self-esteem and sense of purpose over time. Those motivated primarily by career advancement, by contrast, showed short-term benefits but were more likely to discontinue volunteering once the professional goal had been achieved.[/33] The implication, Nwosu argues, is that the psychological benefits of volunteering are contingent on the quality of the underlying motivation.

This distinction has practical implications for charities and organisations that rely on volunteer labour. [34]If the benefits of volunteering are tied to genuine engagement rather than instrumental calculation, then recruitment strategies that emphasise personal development or career advantage may paradoxically attract the kinds of volunteers whose commitment is least durable.[/34] Organisations would do better, Nwosu suggests, to emphasise the value of the cause itself.

The wider social dimension of volunteering is also significant. Communities with high levels of voluntary activity tend to show greater social cohesion, lower rates of isolation, and stronger informal support networks. [35]Volunteering creates connections that cross the boundaries of age, class, and background in ways that few other social activities manage, generating what sociologists call 'bridging capital' — the kind of trust and understanding that forms between people who would not otherwise meet.[/35]

[36]Critics of the volunteering movement sometimes argue that it functions as a substitute for properly funded public services, allowing governments to offload responsibilities onto unpaid citizens. This criticism has genuine force, but it should not be allowed to obscure the independent value of voluntary engagement as a social and psychological good.[/36]

questions.question: What does research about volunteers suggest, according to the article?
options: A) That people are more likely to volunteer if they have experienced personal hardship.
B) That volunteering appears to improve the mental health and wellbeing of those who do it.
C) That the benefits of volunteering are mainly felt by those who receive help.
D) That wellbeing levels among volunteers tend to decline after the first year of service.
questions.explanation: Research shows volunteers 'report higher levels of personal wellbeing than non-volunteers,' suggesting helping others benefits the helper too.

questions.question: What is the key distinction Dr Nwosu makes in her research?
options: A) Between volunteers who work for large charities and those who work for small ones.
B) Between volunteers who are students and those who are already in employment.
C) Between those whose motivation to volunteer is altruistic and those whose motivation is self-interested.
D) Between volunteers in developed countries and those in developing nations.
questions.explanation: Nwosu distinguishes 'contributive' motivation (genuine desire to help) from 'resume' motivation (career advancement), producing different psychological outcomes.

questions.question: What did Dr Nwosu's findings show about volunteers with contributive motivation?
options: A) They experienced lasting improvements in how they felt about themselves over time.
B) They were more likely than others to move into paid roles within charitable organisations.
C) They reported the highest short-term satisfaction but the lowest long-term commitment.
D) They were more effective than other volunteers at raising funds for their organisations.
questions.explanation: Contributively motivated volunteers 'showed sustained improvements in self-esteem and sense of purpose over time,' unlike those motivated by career goals.

questions.question: What problem does Nwosu identify with recruitment strategies that stress career benefits?
options: A) They attract volunteers who are too young and inexperienced for demanding roles.
B) They make it harder for charities to identify volunteers with relevant professional skills.
C) They create tension between long-term and short-term volunteers within organisations.
D) They may recruit people whose commitment is unlikely to last once their personal goal is met.
questions.explanation: Such strategies 'paradoxically attract the kinds of volunteers whose commitment is least durable' — those who stop once their career goal is achieved.

questions.question: What social benefit of volunteering does the article highlight?
options: A) It provides unemployed people with skills needed to re-enter the workforce.
B) It brings together people from different backgrounds who might not otherwise interact.
C) It allows communities to develop independently of government support and funding.
D) It reduces crime rates by giving young people a productive use of their spare time.
questions.explanation: Volunteering creates connections 'that cross the boundaries of age, class, and background,' generating 'bridging capital' between people who would not otherwise meet.

questions.question: How does the article treat the criticism that volunteering replaces properly funded public services?
options: A) It dismisses the criticism as exaggerated and politically motivated.
B) It accepts the criticism and argues that governments must do more to fund services.
C) It acknowledges the criticism has merit but argues it does not negate volunteering's value.
D) It suggests the criticism applies only to certain types of volunteering activity.
questions.explanation: The text says the criticism 'has genuine force, but it should not be allowed to obscure the independent value of voluntary engagement as a social and psychological good.'
